speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel number 8 of my interview with Robert Mendler. You were telling us about getting your friend an affidavit to come to the United States with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I don't want to part with him, because we were very close to each other. We were closer than brothers. He had two brothers. One passed away. One's still alive, I believe. But to me, he was closer to me. And I was closer to him than his own brothers. We lived together. We share every crumb, in camp, with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, finally, I live in Germany. I spent four years in Germany, like everybody else, almost. I had to wait for my quota. Because they put a quota on us come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a little bit of help, here, in the United States. My-- my father's cousin, he lives in New York. His name was Max Reibeisen. He had his own brokerage firm. And he was insurance. He only carried movie stars in-- for his insurance company. He was a very, very wealthy man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- this-- his father was a brother to my grandfather. So my uncle got in touch with him, too. Maybe he can-- maybe he knows some way to speed up my quota.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, meantime, he dates the secretary of Vice President Barkley. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He did-- he was-- he used to date her. And he helped me out a little bit, too, to speed up my quota. But in the meantime, we still wait four years in Germany. I don't think I got very much help. But still, that's not the point. So I spent four years in Germany. Waste four years, for nothing, just waste of-- I spent 10 years of my life down the chute. Which I should be in school, get a good education, or something made out of myself. Meantime, I was nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, can I-- I can't turn the clock back, anymore. So finally, time for me-- time for me to come back-- to come to the United States. I got the visa, everything. So I left Germany, April the 25th, 1949. This was on Pesach. So before we went on the ship, we was-- from Bremerhaven-- everybody, every Jewish person got a-- a bottle of wine, from the Joint, and matzohs, which was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not going to go into too much detail what-- how I live in Germany, because it's too long. It's not important, as far as that, you know? So, coming to the United States, I was happy to come in, as I said.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, my friend from Belgium, one night I get up, and he was gone. Nobody knew what happened to him. Nobody. And I lived with him for so many years. And we were the closest one that could be. He used to do black market. He used to go to Hungary. He used to bring the German marks, because the American soldier needed the German marks to trade in, for dollars and so forth, on different things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And nobody knew what happened. Finally, I get a letter from him, in the Israeli uniform, from Antwerp. He write me only a few words. He lives in Antwerp. He works with Haganah. All these years, he was working with Haganah. And I didn't know. He didn't say a word. So I knew he was alive. Meantime, 1948, he went to Israel. And I didn't hear nothing from him, all these years. And somebody told me was killed, down there, when they fought for independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we always talked about him. He was killed. We never knew he didn't survive. So he was killed. Far-- as far as we were concerned, he was dead. He fall in Israel for independence. So I came to the United States by myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Coming to the United States-- I left Germany, like I say, April the 25th. Took us 10 days to come over here on a ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Coming to the United States, there were 3,000 displaced persons, on the ship, like myself. The name of the ship was SS Marine Jumper. They used to bring the GIs to Germany. Coming back to the United States, they brought the display persons back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were 3,000 display persons on the ship, all kind of nationalities, Polish, Slavish, Russian, Jews, all kinds. Where did the ship leave from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bremerhaven, in Germany. You know, on a transport ship, you sleep downstairs. You eat upstairs, and all the entertainment, in the upstairs. It was a terrible trip. Because the water pound-- the water was rough in April. The ocean was very tough. It was pounding down. Three days before we landed in-- in Boston-- the ship was travelling to Boston, from Bremerhaven to Boston. They were going to go to New Orlean.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we went on strike. We didn't want to go on the ship. The majority of people, we want to go to New York. So they sailed to Boston with us. Because nobody wanted to get on the ship. Who wants to go to New Orlean? We never heard of-- in-- in Germany, in Europe, you only hear, New York, Washington, Chicago. Who heard about Boston, even? Never heard of such a thing. So finally, this captain said we're going to go to Boston. So kitchen is downstairs on the boat. You go for your food. You take your food. Then you go, another staircase, upstairs, and you eat upstairs. So I'm going to-- for my food, one-- three days before we land in Boston. That's all what happened with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm going for my food, with my friend. He lives in Muncie, Indiana. As a matter of fact, we saw-- he was here about a couple of weeks ago. We were very-- we lived in Germany, together, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're going for our food. We talk to each other. And I see people going up the staircase. And I always keep my eyes open. Even today, I always keep my eyes open. Maybe, maybe there's a chance. Maybe I find somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- I got into habit, all these years. I keep my eyes always open. Names, I forget. But if I see a face, I'll never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm going for my food. All of a sudden, I look. I recognize a guy going up the steps. And I-- I said to him, so. And I pointed with my finger. And I start to scream, a Gestapo, a Nazi. And I pointed a finger at him. So people behind him start to jump on him, start to beat him up, a lot of commotion on the ship. They were going to kill him. You know, for us, after the war, to find a Nazi, a big fish like that, you would tear him apart. If I would see him on the deck, I would have dumped him to the ocean.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the fellow, I worked, for him, my first job, from my hometown. In 1939, right when the war broke out, I worked for him. He was so mean to me. He knew me before the war. He was so mean to me. He used to beat me up. For my own mother's sake, I wanted to take revenge on him. And I couldn't put my hand on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, right away, CP came in, police. They put him in quarantine. Meantime, they broke his ribs, the people behind him. And I couldn't touch him, even. And we were going to destroy the ship. We wanted that-- we demand to dump him in the ocean. You know, we were a different kind of people, then. We just got out of camp, don't forget? We still weren't normal. It took me a long, long time before I become a really human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, too. When I came into the United States, I had a lot of trouble. Myself, I beat up, in a store, people. I couldn't come to myself yet. And I was very upset. We were going to destroy the ship. We were banging. We were breaking up the ship. And the captain got scared. He call Boston or someplace. And a few hours later-- actually, this was afternoon-- evening-- a-- a Coast Guard ship pulled in next to my mother ship. They pick him up. They pick me up. And there was another fellow from the town, from Poland. He recognized him. He was a Gestapo chief in his town. And there were two of us against him. They took the three of us on the Coast Guard-- a fast boat-- to Boston. So we landed in Boston. And my-- she was still in-- in the ocean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to Boston. And I don't know how the news spread out. There was a lot of cameramen outside, a photographer. And there was a Polish priest, a little Polish priest. And he-- this fellow, he was coming on a Polish visa. A Polish organization, the church, was bringing with him with a lot of refugees to this country. And he was coming over as a-- as a Polish refugee, because he spoke the language perfectly, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Polish priest come to me. And he call me, in Polish, like they used to call me, 'You dirty Jew.' Like that, to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I swore to God-- I promised myself, when I was liberated, nobody but nobody-- I don't care how big the person is-- nobody gonna to call me 'dirty Jew' again. Because I'm not a dirty Jew. I'm not a-- I am a Jew. I'm very proud. But no, I am not dirty. Nobody call me names. I grabbed this priest. Everything is in Polish. I start to choke him. And I kicked him right between the legs. I know what to do. I went to school for that. I was different person. I was a tough character, believe me. And it was like-- the break-- the riot police broke us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to him, how dare you call me 'dirty Jew?' I'm cleaner than you are. And he is a Nazi. I worked for him. I know who he is. And don't tell me who he is. So I was in Boston, for six days, at a trial with him. He was denying. And we were accusing him. Finally, I was so disgusted.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stay in a hotel. In the meantime, my uncle want to see me. In the meantime, my friend, from Chicago, already landed. He went home to his family. Everybody went to his loved one, whoever had. And I was still in Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to these people, look, I don't care what you do with him. I'm-- I'm all fed up. If you don't believe me-- I want to go home. I want to see-- I want to go to my uncle. I don't care what you do.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't believe me, I said to them, why don't you send-- there was no TV, then. There was TV, but there was no satellite-- send a radio picture of him to my hometown. If it's not the same man, you can send me back. Everything was in Polish, in the immigration office, the trial. So that's what they did. They sent his radio picture to my hometown. The next day, the answer came back. The Polish government was looking for him, because he was one of the biggest war criminal in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they promise they're gonna to send him back. You go home. They put a ticket on me, here, my name. And they told the conductor where I have to get up-- get out. The train was going to New York. Meantime, the-- one of the-- a lady from HIAS used to come every day, used to pick me up. Took me for a walk. Took me for lunch. And when the time was ripe, you know? What name did you enter the United States under?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What name did you enter the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reibeisen, Reibeisen, Romek Reibeisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spent some time in Boston. She was very, very nice. I almost fell in love with her. [LAUGH] I'll be honest with you. She was very nice to me. Because I was lonely, you know, and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a young man. I need somebody. And I could talk to her. I talked a little bit of Yiddish to her. I couldn't speak English. I couldn't understand a single word English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what happened? So finally, the answer came back from my hometown, from the government. They were looking for him, because he killed thousands of people, not only Jews, Poles, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent them to-- to-- to Auschwitz. They killed them all. Single-handed, he killed I don't know how many. So they will send him back. I couldn't believe it. I didn't care. But finally, I sat down. And I wrote a letter to my father's friend in my hometown. He was a judge in our town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't remember his name anymore. It's been so long. So I wrote him a letter. I wanted to know what happened. Well, they brought him back to Poland. But what happened? I told him who I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent me all the paper from my real estate, too. He was very nice to me, because I have some real estate. I sold one piece of property in Poland, you know, when I was here, already. And he sent-- he helped me out a lot. Because he was a very good friend of my father. He used to come to our house, every day, drink the soda water. We had a lot of people come. You know, they were very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he write me a letter. Yeah, they brought him in to our town. It was a short trial. And they hanged him. So from then, I thought to myself, gee, there must be a God, maybe, because my-- was it accident or was it meant to be this way? Or was it planned that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened. So, I thought, there-- there must be probably a God. Because my-- my-- my prayer been answer. That's all I want, the satisfaction from him, to get that. All through this year, when I was in camp, is-- when I lost my faith in the Almighty, I always said, please, God, if there's such a thing, I want to find this man. And let me live. I want to live. I want to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe there's something. I don't know. I'm still not a religious man, today, but I believe in God. Whatever. You know, I have to. I have kids. They have to know who they are. How did you arrive in Latrobe?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, what happened? I-- Well, I came to New York. I live in New York with my aunt, my father's sister. She was alive then. So I stay with her for about 10 days. I didn't like it. They didn't treat me well. They didn't treat me well at all, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I had some cigarettes, because I used to smoke, then. So I'd have a few dollars, because they gave us some dollars on the boat. You know, and I bought cigarettes. What can I buy? Meantime, she was smoking, so she smoked all of my cigarettes. [LAUGH] I shouldn't tell that, really. So I had a tough time in New York, you know? I used to walk around the streets and pick up cigarettes' butt. You know, I had nothing to smoke, because she used all my cigarettes. [NON-ENGLISH] I shouldn't say that, because she was still my aunt, as far as-- regardless.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, my uncle say, you come to Latrobe. I want you to come to Latrobe. Stay with me. I have a room if you want to come. I-- I say, I want to come, right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sent me tickets from Latrobe to New York. Put me on a train. I came to Latrobe. And I was very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard for me to be in Latrobe, because, you know, I didn't speak the language. I used to go to the store. My uncle had a very small store. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned the business very quick. I come from a business family. My father was a businessman. My mother was a businessman. And I used to do business, too, in-- in Germany. I always had a head on my shoulder. I learned the business very quick, sizes and the heels and size and this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of business was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a shoe store. My uncle had a shoe store. I didn't like it, too well, because I was always was used to work outside. I don't like to be inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what can I do? This was my life. And this was my new future. And I had to accept that, which I did. And I'm not sorry. I've become successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, I start to learn how to speak English. I didn't go to school. Well, we had no night school, here, in Latrobe. I'm self-tutored. I have to pat myself on the shoulder, give myself credit, in this respect. Because I didn't go to school, and I learn how to read and write in English. I can speak other languages and read and write, also, but not in-- It was very hard for me from beginning. There's so many different syllable means different things in English. It was very hard on me. But I spoke other languages. I start to learn with the Italian fellows, in Latrobe, till I start to meet my friends in Greensburg, a couple of Jewish fellows. And I start to go out. Settled. I worked for about a year. Bought myself a car. And I start to travel to Pittsburgh, start to date some girls in the-- just a little bit. I date them all, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure. [LAUGHS] Every week, I have three dates, you know? The girl fixed me up with some other girl. It was-- was that. You know, we had a good time. It was very good. And finally, I met my wife on a blind date. And, as soon as I met her, I thought to myself, well, she's different. I think she's-- she's going to be my wife. And she fall in love with me, too. She was young. She's younger than me, a good bit. So when I married her, she was only 19 years old. Her mother had to go sign the paper for me-- for her. I was a little bit older. I was 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married March the 7th, 1954. And I got married at Rodef Shalom Temple. Dr. Freehof married us, a nice wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wasn't too happy with the wedding, this-- a marriage in a-- in a [NON-ENGLISH]. Because I wasn't brought up as a Reform Jew. I was brought up as Orthodox Jews. And this was a little bit different for me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Rabbi Freehof said, I know where you come from. I know who you are. If you want me to, I do anything you want me to do. You want to be like yourself. You want me to wear yarmulke, I'll be happy to. I said, yes, I want you to wear yarmulke. I'm not used-- I-- maybe I'm not doing it for myself. I'm doing it for my-- for my dad, the way I was brought up. I want him to-- if he's such a thing, maybe his soul's watching over me. Let him see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the chuppah, you know? So I promised my wife, when I'm going to be married 25 years to you, I'm going to make a wedding my own, the way I know. The way my ancestor got married and everybody else. So I had a wedding in my backyard, a chuppah and everything, a real you know, an Orthodox wedding. I had it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And, in the meantime-- you know? So, I raised my family. I had a first child, my oldest son, six years later, after we got married. And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Michael, named after my dad, Menachem Zev. My father, his shalom name was Menachem Zev. So I named him after my dad. He's the oldest one. And I had a nice pinyod haben. No, that didn't happen, because my wife was carrying a daughter. I couldn't have pinyod haben. For my grandson, I had a pinyod haben. And six year later, we adopted my second son. His name is Mark, Mark Mendler. Mordechai Chaim. He's named after my wife grandfather. And now I have three grandchildren. My oldest grandson is David Mendler. And the-- my two others-- he lives in Washington. My other two live in Derry, not too far from me. Name is Max Mendler. And we got a new one, about a year and half ago, Mickey Mendler. They're like M and M, [LAUGHTER] so name them M, M.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I am. When--

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become Robert Mendler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert Mendler I become when I-- when I got my citizen papers. The judge-- when I went for-- after five years, I been-- when I was in the United States, I got my citizen papers, like everybody else. First, I was drafted to the army, when I came over, right away. The Korean War broke out. A few months later, and I was drafted. I passed my tests, everything 1-A. I went to Post Office building, and that's where I pass everything. My intelligent-- because I couldn't read English or write, so they gave me the pictures, which I pass. And I was going to my camp, Fort Dix, New Jersey. I go on the train. I was ready to go. Finally, one-- a colonel come around. He called my name and two other fellows. We're in war now. We have no time to teach you, to send you to school. They were going to send me to school, for the intelligence, and back to Germany, use me as intelligence, something like that. I don't know. We have no time. Here's the tickets. Go back home. Which I went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got my papers, my citizen papers. The judge asked me, don't you-- would you like to change your name if you want it to be easy? Everybody called me, Mendler, Mendler, Mendler, because I looked like my uncle, a family resemblance. And everybody thought I am his adopted son, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why don't you take up 'Mendler,' the judge said to me. Be easy, more Americanized. It's hard. Some people call 'Ribinsen,' 'Rabinsen,' 'Rebe.' They couldn't spill-- spell the name right. I said, OK. So people change name all the time. So I took my mother's name. That's why I changed my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you do with the shoe business? And how long were you working in the shoe business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was here about a few weeks, and I learned the business very quick. I couldn't speak, but I sold shoes. We had three movie theaters in Latrobe. I used to go three times a day to movies, to watch the screen, to-- how they talk. And little by little, you know, I learned. Then, I learned from people in the store. They used to start to talk to me. I was forced to learn the language, really. And I learned. I-- in languages, I have no trouble. I always have no trouble with the languages. I pick up very fast. So I learned the language. I'm never going to be perfect, like you, or American-born, like others, because I cannot. I can't be. You only learn when you're young. You get this thing. But still, I want to share-- tell you something, because you were there. Last year, I got the greatest honor was placed upon me. I-- I got a doctor degree in the Seton Hill College, a Catholic university, which they never accept Jewish students before. It was a Christian school. I was the first. I am the first Jew to got degree in this particular college. I'm so proud of it. So, from now on, I can use the title as Dr. Robert Mendler. You were at my--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was an honorary PhD, received from the Center for Catholic Holocaust Studies.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very nice. I am so proud. And I'm very, very active in this school. This is my baby, really. I am their baby, they call me. You know. Anything I can do-- I travel around. I-- I-- I-- I speak in schools, colleges, different institution, wherever I'm invited. I don't turn them down, whenever it's possible for me to go and talk about. The reason I do, because I feel maybe I leave some kind of legacy behind me. People should know what happened, not only to me but to thousands like that, like myself. And time is running out for us. Not too long ago, I was just a youngster. Today, I'm reaching old age, already, almost. I don't feel my age yet. But I act like a 30-years-old kid, believe me. And I feel, too-- and I perform like that, too. But you can't stop progress. Time doesn't stay still, unfortunate for all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that I have left out, or that you would still like to say, or any special thoughts about the Holocaust or your family? Anything that you would like to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I'd like to share--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Don't start.
